patterns:
1. Open by mirroring a specific phrase or tension from the brief in quotation marks or close paraphrase — this single signal proves you read it and immediately separates you from batch applicants.

2. Lead with your single most relevant credential framed around the client's exact context — name the tool, platform, industry, geography, or eligibility requirement they specified, never a generic capability summary.

3. Always include a named "Quick Win" deliverable with a concrete timeline (1–7 days) tied to an obvious pain point — make it specific enough the prospect can picture receiving it tomorrow, not a vague offer to "get started."

4. State 2–3 priorities pulled directly from their brief as a short scannable list — this replaces abstract promises with a visible plan and signals you already think like a strategic partner, not a vendor waiting to be told what to do.

5. Anchor credibility with one precise metric from a named past deliverable — a single number (e.g., "funnel conversion ~34%", "onboarding drop-off reduced ~5%") outperforms paragraphs of capability claims every time.

6. Translate every deliverable into a business outcome — never list outputs alone; always connect them to speed, conversion, reduced friction, or lower churn so the prospect sees ROI, not just work product.

7. Keep the entire message scannable in under 30 seconds — use dashes, bullets, or hard line breaks between sections; a dense paragraph wall kills replies regardless of content quality.

8. When you cannot research the prospect's work, openly name the gap and convert it into engagement — offer a free session or ask for their brief rather than fabricating relevance or concealing the limitation.

9. Match the prospect's tone and register exactly — mirror casual or even typographic informality if their brief is conversational, technical precision if they are engineering-focused; never default to stiff agency formality.

10. Close with a single low-friction call to action — a 15-minute call, a free wireframe, or a no-commitment sample asset — reducing the commitment required to reply to near zero.
